entry.title: Body of missing man recovered from Payette River
entry.summary: A Boise County Sheriff’s office vehicle is pictured in this undated photo. BOISE COUNTY — Search and rescue crews have recovered the body of a man who’s been missing for several weeks. Officials from multiple agencies found and recovered the body of 67-year-old John Barmettler from the Payette River on Wednesday, June 17, the Boise County Sheriff’s Office posted on social media. The recovery ended a search-and-rescue effort that has been ongoing since June 1. Kayakers spotted Barmettler’s abandoned vehicle down a steep embankment near the South Fork of the Payette River on June 1, and it was recovered the next day. In the time since, the search for Barmettler has included crews from several agencies, including the Boise County Sheriff’s Office, Garden Valley Fire & EMS, Boise County Search & Rescue, Bear Valley Rafting, Cascade Raft & Kayak, Idaho Mountain Search & Rescue, Mountain West Commercial Diving, Gem County Towing, Garden Valley Market, “and many community members and volunteers throughout the Garden Valley community,” the sheriff’s office said. “Our thoughts and prayers are with Barmettler’s family and all those whose lives he touched and positively impacted,” the sheriff’s office said on social media. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. BOISE COUNTY — The Boise County Sheriff’s Office is seeking the public’s assistance in locating a man whose vehicle was found unoccupied near … Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. entry.title: Off-duty law enforcement officers, family member killed in Idaho crash
entry.summary: First responders work the scene of a crash on U.S. Highway 12 near Kooskia where three motorcyclists were killed on June 16, 2026. KOOSKIA — Two of the three people killed in a crash Tuesday on an Idaho highway have been identified as Colorado law enforcement officers. The Idaho County Coroner’s Office on Wednesday evening identified the victims of the crash as Jeremy Coleman, 45, of Berthoud, Colorado; Ethan Powers, 35 of Timnath, Colorado; and Nathan McCormick, 26, of Loveland, Colorado. The Colorado Fraternal Order of Police said on social media that Coleman and Powers were a sergeant and deputy, respectively, in the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office, and that McCormick was Coleman’s son-in-law. The Colorado FOP said the law enforcement officers were off duty at the time of the crash. The Larimer County Sheriff’s Office said on social media that the three were vacationing together on a motorcycle trip. “Sgt. Coleman and Deputy Powers dedicated their lives to protecting their community, and their impact will continue to be felt by those who had the privilege of serving alongside them,” the Colorado FOP’s post reads. Neale Brewer, a 60-year-old woman from Colorado Springs, Colorado, has been arrested and charged with three counts of felony vehicular manslaughter, according to Idaho State Police, the Idaho County Jail and online court records. She is being held on a $1 million bond. ISP stated that Brewer was driving a 2019 Ford F-150 westbound on U.S. Highway 12, near Kooskia, at about 4 p.m. Tuesday, June 16, when she allegedly attempted to pass another vehicle in a no-passing zone. The pickup crossed the double yellow center line and collided with three motorcycles traveling eastbound. All three motorcyclists died at the scene. Brewer was transported to a local hospital for medical evaluation and was arrested following her release. “There are no good words in a situation like this,” the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office said on social media. “Put simply, these were incredible people who dedicated their lives to making Larimer County better every single day. They trained hard, they served well, and they loved life. As we walk the long and winding path of grief alongside their families, our resolve is stronger than ever.” Larimer County is located in northern Colorado and encompasses cities that include Fort Collins and Loveland. Kooskia is a small town in north-central Idaho, located about 75 miles east of Lewiston. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. A Colorado woman faces vehicular manslaughter charges after she allegedly killed three motorcyclists while passing in a no-passing zone. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. entry.title: Gov. Little says 'Idaho is prepared' as drought, low snowpack mark start of fire season
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Nampa Fire Protection District Chief Kirk Carpenter, president of the Idaho Fire Chiefs Association, adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. BOISE — Firefighting agencies gathered Tuesday at the Idaho State Capitol for a pre-season wildfire briefing. As fire season enters into its early stages, state leaders underscored that the historically hot and dry conditions bring the potential for a significant and costly fire season. Officials who spoke at Tuesday’s Land Board meeting and subsequent pre-fire season press conference consistently said widespread drought conditions are expected to continue, conducive to a heightened fire season. As of Tuesday — still very early in the fire season — there have been 59 fires across 549 acres, 26 of which were human caused, 13 were natural and 20 have a cause yet to be determined, the Idaho Department of Lands’ (IDL’s) division administrator of operations Jake Strohmeyer said. Gov. Brad Little delivers a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. While the winter months saw near-normal precipitation, warm temperatures resulted in above-normal snowpack only being observed at elevations above 6,000 feet near the panhandle and 7,000 feet in southern Idaho. Jim Wallmann, a Bureau of Land Management meteorologist at the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC), said these temperatures were the second warmest on record, averaging 6 degrees above normal from December through May. The limited snowpack in Idaho, which was at a record low as of April 1, has impacted the fire fuels in the state, with conditions characterized as “above normal” for much of southern Idaho. After a wet April in southwest Idaho, an abundance of fine fuels, such as tall grasses, have grown in the region, Wallmann said. If the dry and hot conditions continue as expected, these fuels only add to the potential for future wildfires. In some respects, Wallmann said what Idaho is observing is not unique. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, about 60% of the country is experiencing drought conditions. Though these conditions are starting to show signs of improvement in the southeastern U.S., the snow drought in the western U.S. is contributing to worsening drought conditions. The most recent national seasonal drought forecast indicated that drought will persist through much of Idaho at least through to the end of August. In April, the Idaho Department of Water Resources issued an emergency drought declaration for each of the state’s 44 counties. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, Owyhee and Twin Falls counties are experiencing “exceptional drought,” the highest intensity on the drought scale. Though conditions conducive to wildfire are expected to persist over the coming months, officials reiterated IDL was in a positive staffing position before fire season gets into full swing. Across IDL’s 50-engine fleet, Strohmeyer said 36 will be fully staffed, 11 more than the previous fire season. In terms of individual personnel, the department has 260 operational firefighters heading into the fire season, 31 more than last year. He added that 48% of these firefighters have one or more years of fire experience and all of the department’s full-time firefighting positions have been filled. The department is also bringing back a Type 1 helicopter based out of Coeur d’Alene which just came under contract, but is still working to bolster its air resources for the future. While IDL works to restart its helitack program, which provides aerial support for wildfires, the department will be partnering with the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources to bring on their Type 3 helicopter along with their crew. Strohmeyer said this arrangement will allow firefighters to receive needed training for when IDL’s helitack program restarts, potentially as soon as next year. Idaho Department of Lands Director Dustin Miller adds to a detailed update on preparedness for the 2026 fire season during a press conference outside the Capitol building, Tuesday, June 16, 2026. IDL Director Dustin Miller further highlighted 11 IDL wildfire detection cameras were operating across the state, with eight additional cameras due to be installed this year. This comes in addition to the official adoption of “Ready, Set, Go!” as the state’s evacuation messaging framework to ensure consistent standards are in place when communicating wildfire response. “If we aren’t the first, we’re one of the first states in the United States to actually have a statewide mass evacuation protocol,” Nampa Fire Protection District Chief and President of the Idaho Fire Chiefs Association Kirk Carpenter said, “and we can only do that when we have relationships that span across to all state and federal partnerships.”

entry.title: Federal judge blocks parts of Idaho bathroom bill from taking effect
entry.summary: Idaho lawmakers passed a sweeping bathroom ban March 27, 2026, approving legislation that would make it a crime for transgender people to use the bathroom that matches their gender identity, even inside privately owned businesses. The bill that would have criminalized transgender people for using public restrooms consistent with their gender identity will not fully go into effect July 1, after a federal judge granted injunctive relief Tuesday to Idahoans who sued to block the implementation of that aspect of the law. House Bill 752, which Gov. Brad Little signed earlier this spring, labeled the use of bathrooms or changing rooms “designated for use by the opposite biological sex” as a misdemeanor for the first instance and a felony punishable by up to five years’ imprisonment for the second. The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and its Idaho chapter, as well as Lambda Legal, represent six transgender Idaho plaintiffs in the case known as Jackson-Edney v. Labrador. The plaintiffs are not challenging the changing room aspect of the legislation, and the injunction blocks the ban from taking effect with regards to single-user public restrooms. “This ruling means trans folks in Idaho can continue participating in public life without the threat of being arrested for using the bathroom,” the ACLU of Idaho’s legal director, Paul Carlos Southwick, said in a statement. “This ruling will relieve that anxiety for our trans friends and neighbors.” Vagueness was one ground on which enforcement of the statute was blocked. “The Court finds that H.B. 752 is unconstitutionally vague because it fails to provide sufficient standards for law enforcement,” Judge Amanda Brailsford’s court order read. For example, the terms “dire need” and “reasonably available” are used in reference to circumstances in which exceptions can be made to the ban, but the legislation’s text does not give definitions for either term. The definition of an individual’s biological sex — whether it is determined by an officer’s estimation of one’s visual appearance or by DNA collection that cannot be mandated without a warrant — was another issue the ruling cast as vague and arbitrary. “Without statutory definitions, narrowing context, or settled legal meanings H.B. 752 invites arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement,” the order reads. “Having concluded that Plaintiffs are likely to succeed on the merits of their vagueness claim, the Court finds irreparable harm will likely occur in the absence of preliminary relief.” The attorney general’s office had argued in favor of HB 752 on the basis of “privacy and safety concerns that have always motivated sex-segregated bathrooms,” as Solicitor General Michael Zarian told the court June 5. Brailsford said that Idaho already has laws addressing misconduct in restrooms, such as those against sexual battery, malicious harassment and indecent exposure. In a Tuesday statement, Attorney General Raúl Labrador said that the ruling was a misinterpretation of HB 752’s intent and the state’s position. “Biological sex is not vague, and neither is the law,” Labrador said. “The good news is that the ruling is narrow. Idaho’s law remains enforceable in most settings, including changing rooms and many restrooms.” The law may still be enforced with respect to multiple-stall restrooms if no single-stall facility is available. Labrador expressed his intention to appeal the decision, which remains in place as the court continues to weigh HB 752’s constitutionality. Six transgender plaintiffs are suing to prevent Idaho''s new bathroom law from being enacted July 1. They are being represented by the ACLU. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left.

entry.title: David W. Hahn named lone finalist to become Boise State University's next president
entry.summary: David W. Hahn, lone finalist to be the eighth president at Boise State University. BOISE — After a yearlong search, Boise State University has found its new president. The Idaho State Board of Education on Tuesday announced David W. Hahn as the lone finalist to serve as the eighth president of Boise State University. Hahn will succeed Marlene Tromp, who in March 2025 announced she was leaving Boise to become president of the University of Vermont. Jeremiah Shinn, Boise State’s vice president for student affairs and enrollment management, served as interim president, though in April was tabbed by the University of Montana to be its next president. Nancy Glenn, vice president of research and economic development, stepped in as acting president while the search at Boise State continued. Hahn currently serves as the Craig M. Berge Dean of the College of Engineering at the University of Arizona. “After a rigorous and thorough national search, I am confident the State Board has identified the right leader for this moment at Boise State,” David Turnbull, chairman of the Presidential Search Committee and member of the Idaho State Board of Education, said in a press statement. “Dr. Hahn brings exceptional credentials as someone who has consistently built institutions that are stronger and more innovative than when he arrived. Boise State and the state of Idaho are well-served by this selection.” Hahn brings more than 30 years of combined experience in engineering education, research and administration. “Boise State University has exceptional momentum, and my pledge is to continue guiding our contributions in all aspects, from a strong commitment to delivering a high-value and affordable education to our students, innovating in support of state, regional and national research needs, partnering with the communities and industries of Idaho, fostering the winning culture of Bronco Athletics, while successfully navigating the changing landscape of higher education,” Hahn said in a press statement. “This all starts with people and throughout my interactions, I have seen a group fully dedicated to the success of Boise State University and I am honored to join the team.” In accordance with Idaho state law, Hahn was named as the sole finalist for the position, officially beginning a 10-business-day period prior to a formal vote by the Idaho State Board of Education. You can email the Idaho State Board of Education with your feedback at . Trevor Hawes is the executive editor of the Idaho Press. BOISE — Ten days remain in the countdown to vote on Boise State University’s next president, with David W. Hahn named as the sole finalist for… Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Gov. Little announces new working group for university athletics programs
entry.summary: Members of the Boise State football team run a drill this spring during a practice at Albertsons Stadium. BOISE — Gov. Brad Little announced the formation of a new College Athletics Working Group on Monday, with a stated aim of keeping Idaho universities’ athletics programs competitive. The group will be hearing from each Idaho public university’s athletic department over the coming months to identify both “policy solutions and investment opportunities tailored to each institution’s needs,” a news release from Little’s office said. “Idaho’s colleges and universities deliver real value to students, communities, our economy, and our athletics programs are a meaningful part of that. This working group will make sure we’re not sitting on the sidelines,” Little said in the release. The working group is set to be chaired by Cortney Liddiard, an Idaho Falls businessman and former CEO of commercial real estate firm Ball Ventures. The remaining board members, comprised of educational, business and legislative leadership, is as follows: The working group arrives as the White House and Congress are exploring federal reforms to college sports. While the release notes these efforts are pending, the working group will focus on options at the state and local levels that can serve to benefit public universities. The group is expected to present its findings to Little in September along with recommendations for how to best support the “long-term competitiveness and sustainability of intercollegiate athletics in Idaho,” the release said. The working group arrives several years after the NCAA instituted a Name, Image and Likeness (NIL) policy in 2021 to permit student-athletes to make money off of their personal brand after previously being forbidden from doing so. Today, these student-athlete deals still lack federal regulation and have instead been met with a patchwork of state regulations that lack consistency in how NIL arrangements are allowed to operate. While the majority of states have since enacted respective state legislation, Idaho is one of more than a dozen without statewide rules. In a show of support for federal NIL regulation, the Idaho Legislature approved Senate Joint Memorial 114 this past legislative session, urging Congress to take “action to prevent program cuts, escalating costs, and competitive disparities.” While Idaho’s joint memorial was being debated, representatives for Boise State offered support for national regulation of these deals, contending that the current arrangement has resulted in colleges in other states playing by different rules. Currently, states that go without regulation of NIL deals operate under rules adopted by an individual university and the NCAA’s overarching regulatory framework. That framework shifted significantly last year, after a federal lawsuit was settled to allow schools to directly share revenue with student-athletes, among other changes, as was reported by the Associated Press. “We need a single uniform national framework for NIL, revenue sharing and eligibility status to preempt state laws and litigation,” Boise State athletic director Jeramiah Dickey said in prior testimony on the legislature’s memorial. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: Latest CDC data puts Idaho town among nation's top COVID wastewater hot spots
entry.summary: Signs lead people to a COVID-19 vaccination clinic setup at a park in Caldwell. The COVID-19 pandemic may feel like a distant memory for most Pocatello residents, but something unusual is showing up in the city’s wastewater — and public health officials are paying attention. According to data released earlier this month by the CDC’s National Wastewater Surveillance System, Bannock County is currently listed as having very high levels of COVID-19 in its wastewater — the highest category on the CDC’s five-tier scale. It’s worth noting that the CDC’s designation for Bannock County is based entirely on testing conducted in Pocatello, which is the only city in the county participating in the national surveillance program. No other communities in Bannock County are being tested. Whether the rest of the county mirrors what Pocatello’s wastewater is showing is unknown — though given that Pocatello is by far the county’s largest city and its regional hub, the numbers are likely representative of broader trends in the area. Pocatello is one of only five sites in the entire country currently showing very high COVID levels in wastewater, making it a significant outlier not just within Idaho but nationally. The readings are striking not only because they stand in sharp contrast to the low COVID activity being reported locally in terms of actual cases and hospitalizations, but because the rest of Idaho is showing low to very low COVID levels at its other four wastewater testing sites. With COVID activity low in most areas of the country as of early June, Pocatello’s very high reading stands out as one of the most elevated in the nation. “Very high” is the CDC’s most severe wastewater classification, indicating the highest measurable concentration of COVID viral material in the wastewater system. Wastewater monitoring can detect the virus spreading from person to person within a community earlier than clinical testing and before sick people go to their doctor or hospital — and it can also detect infections in people who have no symptoms at all. Idaho currently has five locations that test wastewater for COVID-19. Two are in Ada County, one is in Latah County and one is in Kootenai County. Pocatello — the county seat and largest city in Bannock County — is the fifth testing site and currently the only one in the state showing very high levels. The disconnect between the wastewater data and the reported case numbers is puzzling but not necessarily alarming — at least not yet. Southeastern Idaho has reported approximately 115 COVID cases since mid-November 2025, with fewer than 10 cases reported in all of May. Local health officials are currently characterizing COVID activity in the region as low. No surge in hospitalizations has been reported. That gap between high wastewater readings and low reported cases isn’t unusual and can happen for several reasons. People infected with COVID shed the virus in their feces — sometimes for days before developing symptoms or ever seeking testing. Historical trends show that when COVID levels increase in wastewater, COVID infections in the community typically increase five to seven days later. In other words, wastewater is an early warning system — not a current case count. The low case numbers may also simply reflect the reality that fewer people are getting tested for COVID than during the height of the pandemic. Home tests don’t get reported, and many people with mild symptoms never seek medical care. The COVID circulating in the community may be more widespread than the official case count suggests. Pocatello’s elevated readings are also coming at a time when COVID is quietly on the rise nationally. As of early June 2026, the CDC has noted it remains possible that there could be larger increases this summer, particularly if a variant that the immune system no longer recognizes becomes more common. Summer COVID spikes have become a regular pattern in recent years, with the highest levels typically concentrated in the Western United States. The good news, public health experts note, is that deaths and hospitalizations have been gradually declining across the U.S. each season even as COVID continues to circulate. Local health officials have not yet issued any precautions to Southeast Idaho residents in response to the very high COVID levels in Pocatello’s wastewater. But given the elevated wastewater readings, local residents should take some basic precautions — particularly those who are elderly, immunocompromised or have underlying health conditions. Staying current on COVID vaccinations and updated boosters is the most important step. Those at higher risk should consult with their healthcare provider about whether additional precautions make sense for their situation. Masking in crowded indoor settings is worth considering, particularly when visiting vulnerable family members. If you feel sick, test before attending gatherings. COVID tests remain widely available at pharmacies and clinics throughout the region. Improving ventilation in indoor spaces when possible also reduces risk. The very high wastewater reading in Pocatello is an anomaly that warrants monitoring. Whether it signals a coming uptick in cases — as wastewater data historically has — or represents a temporary spike that recedes without a corresponding wave of illness remains to be seen. Increased COVID wastewater levels indicate a higher risk of infection, but the data alone cannot tell us how many people are infected or predict with certainty what comes next. What public health officials and residents can do is watch the numbers closely, take reasonable precautions and remember that the wastewater system is telling us something — even if we’re not entirely sure yet what that something means. For up-to-date COVID wastewater data, visit the CDC’s National Wastewater Surveillance System at cdc.gov/nwss. Southeastern Idaho Public Health can be reached at 208-233-9080. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now!

entry.title: Idaho Surface Water Coalition says severely needed water supply ‘simply is not there’
entry.summary: An irrigation system provides water to East Idaho farm land in this file photo. Originally published June 12 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. With farmers entering an important part of the irrigation season, members of the Idaho Surface Water Coalition say they are reducing water deliveries and usage due to a historically low snowpack, a depleted aquifer and drought emergency. The water that farmers and communities need “simply is not there,” the Idaho Surface Water Coalition said in an announcement Thursday that details how members of the coalition are reducing their own water by up to 33% compared to normal water allocations. “Idaho is in a bad situation,” Idaho Surface Water Coalition Chairman Alan Hansten said in the announcement. “The snow never fell this past winter, so now we are dealing with one of the most challenging water years in generations, with the consequences already stacking up.” “Our members are facing deep cuts to each of their water supplies, which in turn is leaving everyone who relies on our water working hard to stretch their supply,” Hansten added. “Farmers are already having to decide what fields to abandon to make it through the summer. The impacts of this very real drought already equate to lost crops and lost income and will be felt in our towns, our businesses, our grocery stores and around kitchen tables across Idaho and our nation.” The Idaho Surface Water Coalition was formed in 2005 by a group of canal companies and irrigation districts. Collectively, the coalition’s members are responsible for delivering water from the Snake River to about 550,000 acres of farmland in Idaho, the coalition said. The water coalition said the water shortfall is forcing the Twin Falls Canal Co. to reduce its water delivery by 33.3% this year, while the North Side Canal Co. and American Falls Reservoir District No. 2 are each reducing their water by 20% this year. Additionally, the Minidoka Irrigation District is reducing its water delivery by 15% this year, while the Milner Irrigation District is reducing water by 12%. As a result, in one instance, a farmer who receives water from Twin Falls Canal Co. is now chopping grain crops early to sell for use as cattle feed rather than let the crop fail later on due to water shortages, the Idaho Surface Water Coalition said. “It’s important that our fellow Idahoans understand the constraints our canal companies, our irrigation districts, our farmers, our ranchers and all water users are facing,” Hansten said. “Every water user is being asked to do more with less, and they are responding with the same resilience that has always defined Idahoans. The path forward is uncertain, but we will continue to work together to do what is best for the state of Idaho.” Coalition members said that while surface water users are feeling the most immediate impact of the water shortage, drought, low snowpack levels and a depleted Eastern Snake Plain Aquifer affect all water users. Snowpack levels are important because when the snow melts in the spring, it flows down from the mountains to fill the rivers and reservoirs with water that farmers depend on, the coalition said. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. Search the complete digital archives for all papers in the Pioneer News Group. Idaho Press 1618 N. Midland Blvd. Nampa, ID 83651 Phone: 208-467-9251 Email: Contact Us

entry.title: How an Idaho state agency stores decades of old government records and artifacts
entry.summary: Idaho State Historical Society Reference Archivist Owen Prout walks through the agency’s vault of historical records on May 21, 2026. Originally published June 15 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. The first few pages of Idaho’s original state Constitution are tattered. Red ink has bled through — a consequence of state archivists deciding to laminate the first few pages in the ‘70s. But even though the state’s foundational legal document is showing wear, it’s available for the public to see — like thousands of other state government records and donated records that the Idaho State Historical Society houses. The original copy of the Idaho state Constitution, with bleeding red ink and tattered pages. As the state shifts away from aging storage facilities elsewhere in Boise, the State Historical Society is building an expanded 50,000-square-foot storage facility at its headquarters in Boise near the Old Idaho Penitentiary, a prison that operated during Idaho’s territorial days and is now open to the public for tours. That new facility, set to be finished in December, will house physical artifacts and tens of thousands of feet of physical records that the agency houses for other state agencies. “Everything we do here is to provide access and to preserve,” said Angie Davis, an outreach and collections archivist with the State Historical Society. But those two values, she added, “often are at odds with each other, because the more access you have to a thing, the more at risk potentially a thing can be.” The state’s Constitution gets checked out so often that staff with the agency made several other copies of it — including a digitally scanned version of the state Constitution, where those red smudges show up. But “if somebody insists on seeing the original Constitution, we will pull it and just take very special care of it,” Davis said. The Idaho State Historical Society, a state government agency that runs the state’s museum, has for decades cared for many records in the government’s possession and donated by private individuals. First founded as a nonprofit in 1881, the organization became a state agency in 1907. Idaho State Historical Society Executive Director Janet Gallimore reflects on her agency’s mission in the Lincoln Reading Room, where members of the public can access historical records. Since Idaho nuclear downwinders became eligible for federal compensation for sickness after nuclear bomb tests, hundreds have turned to the Idaho State Historical Society and other libraries to find records that prove they or their families lived in Idaho decades ago. But some Idahoans might not know about the wealth of records — like decades of old newspapers, including from abandoned mining towns; city records; and records from Idaho’s territorial days — that the state agency stores and makes publicly accessible. Idaho State Historical Society Executive Director Janet Gallimore said history organizations are among the most trusted in the U.S. because they house original materials. So, she said her staff are cautious to stick to the facts. “We’re very careful that when we do interpretation, that we are speaking from the evidence that we have, and we don’t take a stand one way or the other about the facts,” she said. “It’s just — these are the facts, and they provide context for where we are today. But we don’t try to influence people’s perspective. We try to provide context for where we are.” At the entry to the Idaho State Archives‘ office, there’s an exhibit dedicated to Lincoln — built largely with materials donated by former Idaho Attorney General David Leroy. In the 1960s, archivists with the Idaho State Historical Society started preserving copies of newspapers from across the state. Now, the historical society says it has over 40,000 rolls of microfilm — essentially scanned physical archives of newspapers — dating back to 1863. Newspaper articles between 1836 and 1925 can be accessed digitally through the Idaho Digital Newspaper Project. The historical society also supplies public libraries and universities with microfilm. Idaho State Historical Society Outreach and Collections Archivist Angie Davis thumbs through the Idaho Organic Act, an 1863 congressional bill that established a territorial government for Idaho. And the agency has reams of early documents of Idaho’s history, like a copy of the congressional bill that established a territorial government for Idaho, which is riddled with notes along the margins; and a copy of former U.S. President Abraham Lincoln’s order appointing Idaho’s first territorial governor, William H. Wallace. The agency was also donated archival plans for the construction of the Idaho State Capitol building by the firm Hummel Architects. But if the Idaho State Historical Society doesn’t have what you’re looking for, staff often try to help you track it down elsewhere, Davis said.

entry.title: Idaho farmers are hoping Congress can get updated farm bill across finish line
entry.summary: Idaho farmers are hoping Congress will pass an updated farm bill to update safety-net, conservation, financing and other programs. The bill was passed by the U.S. House and is under consideration in the U.S. Senate. Idaho farmers are hoping Congress will pass an updated farm bill to update safety-net, conservation, financing and other programs. Originally published June 11 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. Many of Idaho’s farmers and producers are dealing with skyrocketing costs to do business, but many of the nation’s support systems for them are outdated. “We have pressure from the cost of fertilizer, the increased cost of fuels, the effects of tariffs on equipment and parts that we need to buy for our operations, and at the same time, the prices that we receive … it hasn’t increased in line with the cost of production,” said Jamie Kress, an East Idaho dryland farmer and president of the National Association of Wheat Growers. Kress and other members of Idaho’s agricultural industry are advocating for Congress to approve an updated farm bill — a massive piece of federal legislation that addresses policies such as food assistance like the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, low-interest farm loans, crop insurance, research, conservation programs, and rural development. The 2026 bill re-authorizes or increases funding for some of these programs, such as assistance for farmers like crop loss programs and financial assistance for purchasing agricultural lands and incentives for conservation programs. The U.S. House of Representatives passed a 2026 farm bill, which would update the policies and extend them for five years. Idaho’s Republican U.S. Reps. Mike Simpson and Russ Fulcher voted in favor of the updated bill, which passed in a 224-200 vote at the end of April. It’s now under consideration in the U.S. Senate. “I regularly hear from farmers, ranchers, and producers in Idaho, and their main concern over the years has been when Congress will reauthorize the Farm Bill,” Simpson said in an emailed statement. “Thanks to a Republican White House, Senate, and House, we have officially delivered on our promise to provide certainty to those who feed our nation. While this legislation supports and invests in our rural communities, it also strengthens our national security by protecting our domestic food supply.” The last major update to the bill was in 2018, and had typically been updated every five years. In recent years, Congress has approved short-term extensions. “The biggest challenge of operating in an outdated farm bill is just uncertainty in agriculture,” Kress said. “A lot of our decisions are very forward-looking. There’s very little that we do in an impulsive manner, or just thinking about tomorrow … so that not knowing what parameters we’ll be operating under long-term is really difficult.” The 976-page bill approved by the House touches many different agricultural programs, as well as others, such as rural broadband access and telemedicine access. Braden Jensen, governmental affairs director at the Idaho Farm Bureau Federation, said the organization appreciates the broad spectrum of provisions in the bill because they cover Idaho’s broad spectrum of agricultural products. “We recognize that Idaho agriculture is very diverse, growing everything from potatoes, sugar beets, wheat, our hay, our dairy products, cattle, specialty crops, we have a little bit of everything,” Jensen said. “So because of that diversity, really all these different titles in the farm bill matter to our producers.” Jensen said the new bill added specialty crops, such as hops, seed crops and potatoes, to risk management programs. Additionally the bill increases borrowing limits on special agricultural loans to reflect increasing costs to expand or start new operations, he said. Kress, the wheat grower, also highlighted the funding of a program to create and expand export markets outside the U.S., and another that allows groups to share the costs of overseas marketing and promotional activities. In 2025, Idaho agriculture exports generated about $3 billion in receipts, the Idaho Capital Sun previously reported. Most of Idaho’s exports go to Canada and Mexico. Idaho wheat growers export roughly half the state’s annual wheat production, Kress said. The Gem State typically ranks in the top 5 in the nation for wheat production. “The trade title (of the farm bill) is very important to our industry,” Kress said. She also highlighted that the bill not only important to the industry, but also consumers of American food products.
